display_name: A logged PSU/fan alert is historical — check the current state before declaring a hardware failure
context: A power-supply or fan alert appears in the log or in an environment summary and the engineer is assessing hardware health
observation: A PSU or fan fault/alert message exists in the logs, or an environment field shows a past fault flag
naive_interpretation: The power supply or fan has failed and the hardware needs immediate replacement
correct_interpretation: Environmental alerts are frequently transient: a PSU briefly losing input during a feed switchover, an OIR insertion/removal, a fan ramping during a thermal spike, or a momentary sensor read. The log records that an event happened, not that the component is currently bad. Always read the current operational state of the PSU/fan, not just the historical log entry. A component that alerted once and is now 'ok/present/powered' usually experienced a transient, whereas a sustained or repeating fault is the real failure.
why: Environmental logging captures discrete events; a single past entry does not reflect present state
Power feed switchovers, online insertion/removal, and thermal ramps generate legitimate transient alerts during normal operation
The current environment status (present/ok/powered, fan RPM in range) is the authoritative health signal, not the log history
A real failure is distinguished by a sustained fault state or a repeating pattern, not a lone historical alert

confirmation_commands.purpose: Read the current PSU operational state rather than relying on a past log entry
Confirm current fan presence and status to tell a transient ramp from a real failure
Show present power and cooling state to compare against the historical alert

traps: RMAing a PSU or fan on a single historical log entry without checking that it is currently faulted
Treating a transient alert from a feed switchover or OIR as a component failure
Reading only the log and never the current environment status, the authoritative signal
Missing the distinction between a one-time event and a sustained or repeating fault pattern
